summary: This reader is drawn to progression fantasy with creative, crunchy mechanics—especially card- or tier-based systems—and to underdogs who find clever, unconventional paths rather than simply grinding harder. They value genuine literary and philosophical depth, serious tone, and subverted tropes (particularly the chosen-one archetype). They consistently reject dungeon cores, harem content, cultivation/xianxia aesthetics, VR settings, and comedic parody, and show strong fatigue with second-chance/reincarnation plots and generic power-fantasy alpha-male protagonists.

taxonomy.description: Detailed, trackable skill/stat/level systems where mechanical choices feel meaningful and rewarding to follow
Magic or power systems built around cards, decks, or collectible ability tiles
Inventive, non-standard power/magic systems that feel fresh rather than generic stat-and-class fare
Protagonist starts disadvantaged (weak class, detrimental talent, low birth) and succeeds through cleverness or unconventional methods rather than raw grinding
Thematic ambition beyond the power fantasy—introspective worldbuilding, moral complexity, or literary self-awareness
Deliberately playing against or inverting the 'destined hero' archetype in a meaningful way
Significant portion of the story set in a school, academy, or institutional training environment
Earth or a familiar world is suddenly integrated into a game-like system with monsters, levels, and survival stakes
Dark or gritty world tone; stakes feel real; not played for jokes or genre parody
MC is a non-human entity—golem, monster, tree, AI—rather than a human or standard fantasy race
MC is reborn as, or manages, a dungeon—accumulating monsters and traps to defend against adventurers
Protagonist acquires a romantic/sexual harem of partners as a recurring story element
Eastern-inspired cultivation path, martial sects, body tempering, and qi/energy refinement as core progression
Story takes place inside a virtual reality game, either voluntarily or due to being trapped
Protagonist is reborn or sent back in time with memories of a previous life, enabling a deliberate 'redo' plot
Heavy reliance on meta-humor, genre parody, or satirical framing as the primary mode rather than genuine adventure
Primary story focus on a peaceful craft or profession (brewing, farming, cooking, blacksmithing) rather than combat progression
MC rapidly becomes dominant with little genuine intellectual or strategic challenge; alpha-male archetype with no real underdog arc
Significant focus on constructing and managing a settlement, city, or kingdom as a core loop
Protagonist is transported from the real world into a fantasy realm

themes.description: Detailed, trackable skill/stat/level systems where mechanical choices feel meaningful and rewarding to follow
themes.rationale: Nearly every S/A-tier pick features dense, engaging progression mechanics the reader clearly engages with closely

themes.description: Magic or power systems built around cards, decks, or collectible ability tiles
themes.rationale: Three independent A/S-tier picks all feature card-based systems—a level of clustering that signals a specific mechanical preference beyond general creativity

themes.description: Inventive, non-standard power/magic systems that feel fresh rather than generic stat-and-class fare
themes.rationale: Liked books consistently feature mechanically novel systems; F/SKIP books are disproportionately generic stat-and-class designs

themes.description: Protagonist starts disadvantaged (weak class, detrimental talent, low birth) and succeeds through cleverness or unconventional methods rather than raw grinding
themes.rationale: A recurring structural hook in nearly all top-tier picks; absent or inverted in most F-tier picks

themes.description: Thematic ambition beyond the power fantasy—introspective worldbuilding, moral complexity, or literary self-awareness
themes.rationale: Worth the Candle is the only S-tier title that is not also a top crunchy-progression pick; it earns its place entirely on depth. The reader appears to reward books that have something to say.

themes.description: Deliberately playing against or inverting the 'destined hero' archetype in a meaningful way
themes.rationale: Mark of the Fool (rejected mark), Worth the Candle (meta tabletop origin), Enemy of the World (mankind's most hated man as savior) all share this structure and all scored A or S

themes.description: Significant portion of the story set in a school, academy, or institutional training environment
themes.rationale: Multiple A/S picks feature academy arcs; the reader seems to enjoy the structured progression and social dynamics they provide

themes.description: Earth or a familiar world is suddenly integrated into a game-like system with monsters, levels, and survival stakes
themes.rationale: Several A/B picks use this setting, but the reader also DNF'd many generic system-apocalypse books—the tag is an attractor only when paired with strong execution or a unique hook

themes.description: Dark or gritty world tone; stakes feel real; not played for jokes or genre parody
themes.rationale: The grittiest books (A Soldier's Life, Chain of Feathers) scored high; heavily comedic/satirical books (DCC, This Quest is Broken, BuyMort) were F or SKIP

themes.description: MC is a non-human entity—golem, monster, tree, AI—rather than a human or standard fantasy race
themes.rationale: User override: evidence was only B-tier (Tree of Aeons, Threadbare, Large Chests) — not a strong enough positive to count; treated as neutral.

themes.description: MC is reborn as, or manages, a dungeon—accumulating monsters and traps to defend against adventurers
themes.rationale: Multiple clear SKIP signals across unrelated series; the reader passes on dungeon-core premises without reading

themes.description: Protagonist acquires a romantic/sexual harem of partners as a recurring story element
themes.rationale: Consistently SKIPped across many series spanning different settings; blurb language about 'beautiful women' is a reliable reject signal for this reader

themes.description: Eastern-inspired cultivation path, martial sects, body tempering, and qi/energy refinement as core progression
themes.rationale: Every cultivation-flavored entry is SKIP; the reader shows no interest in the aesthetic even when combined with system or LitRPG elements

themes.description: Story takes place inside a virtual reality game, either voluntarily or due to being trapped
themes.rationale: User override: reader strongly dislikes VR/trapped-in-game settings.

themes.description: Protagonist is reborn or sent back in time with memories of a previous life, enabling a deliberate 'redo' plot
themes.rationale: User override: reader likes second-chance/reincarnation plots.

themes.description: Heavy reliance on meta-humor, genre parody, or satirical framing as the primary mode rather than genuine adventure
themes.rationale: Dungeon Crawler Carl—one of the genre's most acclaimed series—was F'd; This Quest is Broken and Sentenced to Troll were also rejected, pointing to an aversion to self-aware comedy as a primary register

themes.description: Primary story focus on a peaceful craft or profession (brewing, farming, cooking, blacksmithing) rather than combat progression
themes.rationale: Battle Mage Farmer, Jake's Magical Market, and Hawkin's Magic Beers are all SKIP; the reader wants meaningful combat stakes alongside any crafting element

themes.description: MC rapidly becomes dominant with little genuine intellectual or strategic challenge; alpha-male archetype with no real underdog arc
themes.rationale: User override: not a dislike of power fantasy itself — some series like The Primal Hunter were just poorly executed. Treated as neutral; execution/other themes decide.

themes.description: Significant focus on constructing and managing a settlement, city, or kingdom as a core loop
themes.rationale: User override: mild dislike of kingdom/settlement-management as a core loop.

themes.description: Protagonist is transported from the real world into a fantasy realm
themes.rationale: Appears with equal frequency in liked and disliked books; it is a neutral genre frame rather than a preference signal—execution and other tags dominate
